It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.

For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.

When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 79 actions; a strong score needs about 71 or fewer. You are 1.1x over the target.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Check current positions after SPACE
seg = current_frame.segmentation
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] in ['N', 'c', 'W', 'G', 'b']:
        b = n['boundary']
        rows = [p[0] for p in b]
        cols = [p[1] for p in b]
        print(f"id={n['id']}, color={n['color']}, pixels={n['pixels']}, bbox=({min(rows)},{min(cols)})-({max(rows)},{max(cols)}), children={n['children']}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
After SPACE, the white strip and carried square merged back together:
- White strip: W at (31,40)-(35,43) with child b at (33,41)-(34,42)
This is the same state as before I pressed SPACE. So SPACE toggles the white strip between attached and detached states.
Let me think about this differently. The player is at (28,40)-(30,43), and the merged object is at (24,44)-(31,47). The white strip with carried square is at (31,40)-(35,43).
The player can't move RIGHT because the merged object is blocking. The white strip is below the player.
Let me try moving UP to get the player above the merged object, then move RIGHT to get the white strip aligned with the merged object, then move DOWN to merge.
Wait, I already tried that earlier and it resulted in the merged object splitting. Let me try a different approach.
What if I move LEFT, then UP, then RIGHT multiple times to get the white strip above the merged object, then move DOWN to merge?
Actually, let me reconsider the goal. Maybe the goal is not to merge all squares. Let me look at the initial state again.
Actually, I realize I should look at what happens when I move the player into the merged object. Earlier, when I moved RIGHT, the white strip merged with the top part of the merged object, creating a gray frame. But then when I moved DOWN, the merged object went back to its original state.
Maybe the goal is to merge all three squares into the merged object, creating a single object with 3 blue centers. Let me try a different sequence.
Let me try: UP, UP, RIGHT, DOWN, DOWN. This would move the player above the merged object, then move RIGHT to align the white strip with the merged object, then move DOWN to merge.
Wait, but I already tried something like this and it didn't work. Let me think more carefully.
The player is at (28,40)-(30,43). The merged object is at (24,44)-(31,47). The white strip is at (31,40)-(35,43).
If I move UP twice, the player would be at (26,40)-(28,43), and the white strip would be at (29,40)-(33,43). The white strip would overlap with the merged object at rows 29-31, cols 44-43 (but col 43 < col 44, so no overlap).
If I then move RIGHT, the player would try to enter the merged object's space. The white strip would move to (29,41)-(33,44), which overlaps with the merged object at col 44.
Hmm, but earlier when I moved RIGHT, the player couldn't move because the merged object was blocking. Let me check if the player can move into the merged object's space.
Actually, looking at the earlier state, when the player was at (28,40)-(30,43) and I moved RIGHT, the player moved to (28,41)-(30,44) and the white strip moved to (31,41)-(35,44). But the result said `gameplay_changed: true` with only 1 cell changed, which suggests the player did move but the white strip didn't merge.
Wait, let me re-examine. After the first RIGHT from (28,40), the player was still at (28,40)-(30,43) according to the segmentation. So the player didn't move.
Actually, I'm getting confused. Let me just try moving UP, then RIGHT, then DOWN and see what happens.
